Name: asynchronous_memory_access_attention
Title: Asynchronous Memory Access Optimization for Attention Mechanisms in LLM Inference
Short Hypothesis: Introducing asynchronous memory access patterns in attention mechanisms can reduce GPU stalls and improve memory bandwidth utilization, leading to measurable improvements in latency and throughput for LLM inference.
Related Work: Existing work on attention optimization focuses on kernel fusion (e.g., FlashAttention) and low-bit quantization (e.g., BitDecoding). While these approaches improve efficiency, they largely rely on synchronous memory access patterns. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly optimizing asynchronous memory access, a relatively unexplored direction in attention mechanisms.
Abstract: Attention mechanisms in large language models (LLMs) are bottlenecked by memory bandwidth and latency due to synchronous memory access patterns. This work proposes a novel approach to optimize attention mechanisms by introducing asynchronous memory access strategies. Specifically, we design asynchronous kernels for attention score computation and KV-cache retrieval, reducing GPU stalls and improving bandwidth utilization. We implement these kernels in CUDA/Triton and evaluate their impact on latency, throughput, and memory efficiency using profiler metrics such as warp stall reasons and achieved bandwidth. Experiments on LLaMA and GPT models demonstrate significant improvements in tokens-per-second and p50/p99 latency, validated on multiple GPU architectures (Ampere, Hopper). Our results highlight the potential of asynchronous memory access as a key optimization for LLM inference.
Experiments: Implement asynchronous memory access kernels for attention score computation and KV-cache retrieval using CUDA/Triton.
Profile baseline (synchronous) and proposed (asynchronous) kernels on LLaMA-3.1-8B and GPT-4 using NVIDIA Nsight Compute to measure warp stalls, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity.
Compare latency (p50/p99) and throughput (tokens-per-second) between synchronous and asynchronous kernels under fixed accuracy budgets.
Evaluate energy efficiency (energy per token) and scalability under batching for both approaches.
Validate results across multiple GPU architectures (Ampere, Hopper) to ensure generalizability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Asynchronous memory access may introduce complexity in kernel design, potentially leading to debugging challenges.
The benefits of asynchronous access may vary depending on the specific attention variant (e.g., sparse vs. dense attention).
The approach may require significant tuning for different GPU architectures, limiting immediate deployability.
There is a risk of overfitting optimizations to specific hardware configurations, reducing generalizability.
